The paper was ¢xceptionally lucky in finding a wonderful sympathy
hetween its artists and its engravers. The famuly of Grey were crafts-
men of tho most fimished type. Dublin never possessed al group of en-
gravers Who could turn out more conscientious or more beautiful work
than tho brothers James, Alired, and Gregor,. Groey and their father
George Grey. There was not a line or point of John O’Shea’s fine draw-
ings on the Llock that over escaped the unerring touch of these keen-
sighted and skilful masters of their art. If i the days to come any-
body should be curious to see what Dublin could do once upon a time
in the way «cff straightforward wood engraving before the era of process
printing st in no better f(}lllltilllll! of reference could be reached ’th'ul

the cartoons of ‘* Zozimus.’

lack of equipment on its artistic side. On®its literary side it was
equally strong. It had fruitful réserves upon whom it could call at

smmense  demonstrations were written by him for the *‘Cork
Examiner,”’ and afterwards copied into the Dublin and English press.

0'Neill Daunt in his ““Ireland and her Agitators,” the last edition of

)

which appeared in 1888 as ‘85 years of Irish History,” pays a fine
sribute to Lenihan for his services as a political organiser and journalist.
The ‘‘Liberator”’ warmly admired him, and when. Lenihan founded the
« Tipperary Vindicator” at Nenagh in 1844, O’Connell wrote him a
very appreciative Tetter and for half a century this letter was on view
in his office in Limerick, to which city he removed in 1849 on taking

1)

over the ‘‘ Limerick Reporter,” a journal he had previously edited for
two years, 1841-43, when he accepted an engagement from John Francis
Maguire on the staff of tho ‘‘ Cork Examiner.”’ It was during Lenihan’s
editorship of the  Tipperary Vindicator ”’ that he exposed the famous
police plot known as ‘‘ The Shinrone Conspiracy,” and obtained the
dismissal of a large number of constables with their leader.

To ono of his marked hiterary gifts and antiquarian ta,q‘tes Limerick
“had a great attraction, before he set aboub
_collecting material for a history of the city which should supersede the
the smaller works of Ferrar and Fitzgerald and that of J. J. MacGregor.:

- of Dunrwen, Eugene O'Curry, and others, and for several years he wrote

for his paper weekly articles on the subject of the City of the Sieges,

94 THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.

mateorial for a history of Tipperary. & member of his family informeg
me recently that at the time of his death a collection of valuable his-
torical papers mysteriously disappeared. Last year some of hig MSS.
particularly copies of literary lecturcs and historical documents rolating
to Tipperary were sold by auection in Loudon.

Personally, Maurice Lenihan was a well-built, handsome man, of

gentle disposition, and great charm of manner. His one recreation wag

book-hunting. As a book-lover, he was known far and wide, his figure
being as familiar in the bookhaunts of Dublin as in the old city of
the Shannon. The J udge on Assize was even! known to compare notes
with him as to a likely bargain at a metvopolitan hook sale. He pos-
sessed a fine collection of books which when I knew them, were crowned
with the dust of ages. AMany of these ho disposed of towards the end
of his career ta the British Museum. IFor a long period he took part
in tho mundcipal affairs of Limerick, but the honours of the Corporation
seemed to come late as it was only at the beginming of the eightics
that he was appointed High Sheriff and in 1834 Mayor of the anoient

city. He had been made o Justice of tha Peace by his old friend, Lord

Chancellor O’Hagan, and he was an honoured member of the Royal Irish
Academy. Sad to tell, he died very poor, and Limerick has raised no
memoerial stone to mark the grave of the historian so worthy of it8

London. FRANK MacDONAGH.

Decomber, 1841, and cducated at Trinity whera he carried off the polg
medals for oratory and English composition. He graduated M.A, at
-Oxford, entered the church, became curato to ¥. D. Maurice a.nld Wa
advanced to the Canonry in 1891, after acting as 1nsgtructor to the
daughters of RKing Ldward. He married Eleanor, daughter of John
Franas Waller, LL.D., the csteamed poct, sometime eoditor of the
“Dublin University Magazine,”’ aud their eldest son is tho well-known
novelist. For many years the late Canon acted as literary adviser to thg
firm of Cassell and edited “‘Tho Quiver.” He was equally popular as 3
preacher and writer, his works having an extraordinary vogue, the prin-
cipal being: Some Difficulties of Bdlief, 1877 (10 cditions), The Life of
the World to Come, 1878 (6 eds.), St. George for England, Addresses to
Children, 182 (7 <ds.), Translated 1nto French, German and Ttalian.
In October last he published his Recollections ¢f a Royal Chaplain,
which contains many good stories, related with the native humour he
retained to the last. D. J. O’D.
